caption.text: There’s one psychological phenomenon that can kill your SaaS adoption rate,
it’s the gap between intention and action. Let’s take an example,
your user signs up for your invoicing SaaS.
They tell themselves, “I’ll send my first invoice later.”
They close the tab and never come back.
Not because they didn’t want to, or because your product is bad,
but because between the moment the intention was there and the moment
you asked them to take action,
there was way too much distance.
Because forming an intention is easy,
whereas taking action requires effort.
And the problem is, as soon as there’s any delay between the two,
the brain pushes it back to later. And very often, later
becomes never. So to counter this,
identify your user’s intention window.
It’s the exact moment when their motivation is at its peak.
And usually, that window opens right after sign-up.
Your user just created their account, they’re motivated, they’re there, they’re ready.
It’s now or never, not in a follow-up email 24 hours later, or in a
notification 3 days later.
So your onboarding absolutely has to capture that window:
“Add your first client,
it takes 30 seconds.” Then “Create your first invoice, we’ve prepared a template for you.”
One simple action,
immediate, while the intention is still hot.
Because unfortunately, the intention window closes fast, and once it’s closed,
it’s hard to open it again.
